title: Further differentiation and the second derivative
summary: This HL lesson widens your differentiation toolkit and then puts it to work on the shape of a curve. You will differentiate the standard non-polynomial functions ($e^x$, $\ln x$, $\sin x$, $\cos x$), combine them with the chain, product and quotient rules, and then differentiate a second time to get $f''(x)$ (also written $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$). The second derivative reveals concavity — concave up where $f''>0$, concave down where $f''<0$ — and locates points of inflexion where $f''$ is zero and changes sign. Finally it gives the second-derivative test, the fastest way to classify a stationary point: $f''<0$ means a local maximum, $f''>0$ a local minimum. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards marks, because on an exam how you show your working decides how many marks you keep.
learningObjectives: Differentiate the standard functions $e^x$, $\ln x$, $\sin x$ and $\cos x$.
Apply the chain rule to differentiate composite functions such as $e^{g(x)}$ and $\sin(g(x))$.
Apply the product rule $\frac{d}{dx}(uv)=u'v+uv'$ and the quotient rule to combined functions.
Find the second derivative $f''(x)$ (equivalently $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$) and interpret it as the rate of change of the gradient.
Use the sign of $f''(x)$ to determine concavity and to locate points of inflexion where $f''=0$ and $f''$ changes sign.
Classify stationary points with the second-derivative test: $f''<0$ is a local maximum, $f''>0$ is a local minimum.
Present working so that method marks (M), accuracy marks (A) and follow-through (FT) protect the final answer.

simpleExplanation.title: The Rules of Combination, and the Curve of the Curve
simpleExplanation.summary: The chain, product and quotient rules let you differentiate functions that are built by combining simpler ones. The second derivative then differentiates the gradient itself, so it tells you not how steep a curve is but how its steepness is changing — which is exactly what curvature, concavity and points of inflexion are about.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of driving. The first derivative is your speed — how fast the position is changing. The second derivative is your acceleration — how fast the speed is changing. Pressing the accelerator ($f''>0$) makes the graph of your position curve upward (concave up); braking ($f''<0$) curves it downward (concave down). The exact instant you switch from braking to accelerating is a point of inflexion. And the combination rules are just the gearbox: the chain rule for a function inside a function, the product rule for two functions multiplied, the quotient rule for one divided by another.
steps: Recognise the structure: is it a standard function, a function inside a function (chain), a product (product rule), or a quotient (quotient rule)?
Differentiate once to get $f'(x)$, differentiating the parts with the correct rule and simplifying.
Differentiate again to get $f''(x)$ — the second derivative — treating $f'(x)$ as the new function to differentiate.
Read the shape: use the sign of $f''$ for concavity and inflexion, and the value of $f''$ at a stationary point to classify it as a maximum or a minimum.

flashcards.front: Differentiate the four standard functions $e^x$, $\ln x$, $\sin x$, $\cos x$.
flashcards.back: $\frac{d}{dx}e^x=e^x$; $\frac{d}{dx}\ln x=\frac{1}{x}$; $\frac{d}{dx}\sin x=\cos x$; $\frac{d}{dx}\cos x=-\sin x$ (note the minus sign). In AI HL, $x$ is in radians for the trig derivatives.
If $y=f(g(x))$ then $\frac{dy}{dx}=f'(g(x))\cdot g'(x)$: differentiate the outer function, keep the inner function unchanged, then multiply by the derivative of the inner function.
If $y=uv$ then $\frac{dy}{dx}=u'v+uv'$ — 'derivative of the first times the second, plus the first times the derivative of the second'.
If $y=\frac{u}{v}$ then $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{u'v-uv'}{v^2}$. The order in the numerator matters because of the minus sign: it is $u'v-uv'$, not $uv'-u'v$.

It is the derivative of the first derivative: differentiate $f(x)$ to get $f'(x)$, then differentiate $f'(x)$ to get $f''(x)$. It measures the rate of change of the gradient. Also written $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ or $y''$.
The curve is concave up (cup-shaped) there: the gradient $f'(x)$ is increasing.
The curve is concave down (cap-shaped) there: the gradient $f'(x)$ is decreasing.

flashcards.front: What is a point of inflexion?
flashcards.back: A point where the concavity changes. It occurs where $f''(x)=0$ (or is undefined) AND $f''(x)$ changes sign across the point. Just having $f''=0$ is not enough — the sign must actually switch.

flashcards.front: State the second-derivative test for a stationary point.
flashcards.back: At a stationary point $x=a$ (where $f'(a)=0$): if $f''(a)<0$ it is a local maximum; if $f''(a)>0$ it is a local minimum. If $f''(a)=0$ the test is inconclusive — fall back to a first-derivative sign check.

flashcards.front: Common trap: the chain rule and the inner function.
flashcards.back: For $y=\sin(3x)$ you must multiply by the derivative of the inside: $\frac{dy}{dx}=3\cos(3x)$, not $\cos(3x)$. Forgetting the $\times g'(x)$ factor is the most penalised chain-rule error.

flashcards.front: Common trap: $f''=0$ automatically means an inflexion?
flashcards.back: No. $f''=0$ is only a candidate. You must check the sign of $f''$ changes across the point. For example $y=x^4$ has $f''(0)=0$ but $f''\ge 0$ everywhere, so the origin is a minimum, not an inflexion.

flashcards.front: When is the second-derivative test not enough?
flashcards.back: When $f''(a)=0$ at the stationary point it gives no information, so classify using the first-derivative sign change instead (or the GDC graph).

quickCheck.prompt: By the product rule, if $y=uv$ then $\frac{dy}{dx}$ equals:
On an interval where $f''(x)>0$, the graph of $f$ is:
A point of inflexion at $x=a$ requires:
At a stationary point $x=a$ you find $f''(a)=6>0$. The point is a:

faq.q: When should I use the second-derivative test instead of a first-derivative sign check?
faq.a: In AI HL either is acceptable, but the second-derivative test is usually quicker for classifying stationary points because it evaluates $f''$ at a single point rather than testing the sign of $f'$ on both sides. Use it when $f''$ is easy to compute and non-zero at the stationary point. If $f''(a)=0$ the test is inconclusive — then go back to the first-derivative sign check or read the shape off your GDC.

faq.q: What is the difference between a point of inflexion and a stationary point?
faq.a: A stationary point has $f'(x)=0$ (a horizontal tangent). A point of inflexion has $f''(x)=0$ with a sign change (concavity switches). They are independent conditions. A point can be both — a stationary point of inflexion, like $y=x^3$ at the origin, where $f'=0$ and $f''$ changes sign. It can also be an inflexion without being stationary — a non-stationary point of inflexion, where the tangent is not horizontal.

faq.q: Can I use the product rule instead of the quotient rule?
faq.a: Yes. Any quotient $\frac{u}{v}$ can be written $u\,v^{-1}$ and differentiated with the product rule (you will need the chain rule for the $v^{-1}$ part). It gives the same answer and is sometimes tidier, but the quotient rule is usually more direct. Be fluent with both and pick whichever keeps the algebra cleaner.

faq.q: Do the trig derivatives need radians?
faq.a: Yes. The clean results $\frac{d}{dx}\sin x=\cos x$ and $\frac{d}{dx}\cos x=-\sin x$ only hold when $x$ is measured in radians. Make sure your GDC is in radian mode for any calculus involving trigonometric functions.

sections.content: You can already differentiate powers of $x$. This lesson extends that to the other functions the AI HL course uses — the exponential $e^x$, the natural logarithm $\ln x$, and the trigonometric functions $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ — and gives you three rules for combining them: the chain rule for a function inside a function, the product rule for two functions multiplied, and the quotient rule for one divided by another. Then it introduces the second derivative $f''(x)$: differentiate once for the gradient, twice for how the gradient is changing. That second differentiation is what lets you read the curvature of a graph — whether it bends up or down, where it changes bend (a point of inflexion), and, most usefully, whether a stationary point is a maximum or a minimum. As in every calculation topic, each worked example below is laid out exactly the way our engine marks your own practice: the method is on the page, mark by mark.
Derivatives of the standard functions

Before the combination rules you need the derivatives of the individual functions. These four are on your formula booklet, but you should know them cold, along with the crucial detail that the derivative of $\cos x$ carries a minus sign and that the trig results assume radians.

$\dfrac{d}{dx}e^{x}=e^{x}$

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\ln x=\dfrac{1}{x}$

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\sin x=\cos x$

$\dfrac{d}{dx}\cos x=-\sin x$

items: $e^x$ is its own derivative — the only function (up to a constant multiple) with that property.
$\sin x \to \cos x$, but $\cos x \to -\sin x$: the minus sign is a favourite exam trap.
Trig derivatives require $x$ in radians — set your GDC to radian mode.

sections.content: The chain, product and quotient rules

Real functions are usually built from these standard pieces. The chain rule handles a function nested inside another, like $e^{x^2}$ or $\sin(3x)$: differentiate the outer function, keep the inner one unchanged, then multiply by the derivative of the inner function. The product rule handles two functions multiplied, like $x^2\sin x$. The quotient rule handles a fraction, like $\frac{\ln x}{x}$ — and there the order in the numerator matters because of the minus sign.

Chain rule: if $y=f(g(x))$ then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=f'(g(x))\cdot g'(x)$.

Product rule: if $y=uv$ then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=u'v+uv'$.

Quotient rule: if $y=\dfrac{u}{v}$ then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{u'v-uv'}{v^{2}}$.

sections.question:
Differentiate each function.
(a) $y=e^{3x^2}$
(b) $y=x^2\sin x$
(c) $y=\dfrac{\ln x}{x}$  [6]
sections.solution:
**(a) Chain rule.** Outer function $e^{u}$, inner $u=3x^2$ with $u'=6x$.
$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^{3x^2}\cdot 6x=6x\,e^{3x^2}$.  [M1: chain rule with inner derivative, A1: correct]

**(b) Product rule.** Let $u=x^2$ and $v=\sin x$, so $u'=2x$ and $v'=\cos x$.
$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=u'v+uv'=2x\sin x+x^2\cos x$.  [M1: product rule, A1: correct]

**(c) Quotient rule.** Let $u=\ln x$ and $v=x$, so $u'=\dfrac{1}{x}$ and $v'=1$.
$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{u'v-uv'}{v^2}=\dfrac{\frac{1}{x}\cdot x-\ln x\cdot 1}{x^2}=\dfrac{1-\ln x}{x^2}$.  [M1: quotient rule in correct order, A1: simplified]

sections.content: In Paper 1 the formula booklet gives you all three rules, so the marks are not for memorising the formula — they are for recognising which rule applies and identifying the parts correctly. For a chain rule, always ask 'what is the inside?' and remember the $\times g'(x)$ factor. For a quotient, write $u'v-uv'$ (not $uv'-u'v$) and start from $v^2$ in the denominator.
The second derivative and concavity

Differentiate $f(x)$ and you get $f'(x)$, the gradient. Differentiate again and you get the second derivative $f''(x)$ — also written $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ — which measures how the gradient itself is changing. Where the gradient is increasing the curve bends upward; where it is decreasing the curve bends downward. That bending is called concavity, and its sign is read directly off $f''$.

items: $f''(x)>0$: the curve is **concave up** (cup-shaped); the gradient is increasing.
$f''(x)<0$: the curve is **concave down** (cap-shaped); the gradient is decreasing.
A **point of inflexion** is where concavity changes. It requires $f''(x)=0$ (or undefined) AND a change of sign of $f''$ across the point.
$f''=0$ alone is not enough: check the sign actually switches. For $y=x^4$, $f''(0)=0$ but the concavity does not change, so the origin is not an inflexion.

sections.question: For $f(x)=x^3-3x^2-9x+2$, find $f''(x)$, determine the interval on which the curve is concave down, and find the coordinates of the point of inflexion.  [5]
sections.solution:
**First and second derivatives.**
$f'(x)=3x^2-6x-9$, so $f''(x)=6x-6$.  [M1: differentiate twice, A1: $f''(x)=6x-6$]

**Concave down where $f''<0$.**
$6x-6<0\Rightarrow x<1$. So the curve is concave down for $x<1$.  [A1: $x<1$]

**Point of inflexion.** Set $f''(x)=0$: $6x-6=0\Rightarrow x=1$. The sign of $f''$ changes from negative (for $x<1$) to positive (for $x>1$), so it is a genuine inflexion.  [M1: solve $f''=0$ and confirm sign change]
$f(1)=1-3-9+2=-9$, so the point of inflexion is $(1,-9)$.  [A1: coordinates]

sections.content: The second-derivative test for stationary points

The second derivative gives the quickest way to classify a stationary point. At a stationary point the tangent is horizontal, so the curve is turning. If it is concave down there ($f''<0$) it must be turning at a peak — a local maximum. If it is concave up there ($f''>0$) it is turning at a trough — a local minimum. You only evaluate $f''$ at a single point, which is why this test is usually faster than a first-derivative sign check.

items: First solve $f'(x)=0$ to locate the stationary points.
If $f''=0$ the test is **inconclusive** — use a first-derivative sign check (or the GDC) instead.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Dropping the inner-function factor in the chain rule** — $\frac{d}{dx}\sin(3x)=3\cos(3x)$, not $\cos(3x)$. Forgetting the $\times g'(x)$ is the single most penalised chain-rule slip.
**Reversing the quotient-rule numerator** — it is $u'v-uv'$ over $v^2$, not $uv'-u'v$. The minus sign makes the order matter.
**Losing the minus sign on $\cos x$** — $\frac{d}{dx}\cos x=-\sin x$. Writing $+\sin x$ costs the accuracy mark.
**Concluding an inflexion from $f''=0$ alone** — you must also show $f''$ changes sign. $y=x^4$ has $f''(0)=0$ yet no inflexion at the origin.
**Getting concavity backwards** — $f''>0$ is concave up (a minimum-shaped bend); $f''<0$ is concave down (a maximum-shaped bend).
**Trusting the second-derivative test when $f''=0$ at the stationary point** — it is inconclusive there; switch to a first-derivative sign check.
**Working in degrees for trig calculus** — the derivatives $\sin x\to\cos x$, $\cos x\to-\sin x$ only hold in radians; check your GDC mode.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

In an exam the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working. A method mark (M) rewards a correct approach even if the arithmetic later slips; an accuracy mark (A) rewards a correct result and is usually dependent on the method mark being earned. Follow-through (FT) means a correct step performed on your own earlier (wrong) value still scores, and equivalent correct forms are accepted. All of that protection exists only if your method is on the page. Study how each mark below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: For $y=x^3-6x^2+5$, find $\frac{dy}{dx}$ and $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$, and hence find and classify the stationary points.  [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

**Differentiate once.**
$\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3x^2-12x$.

**Find the stationary points.** Set $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=0$:
$3x^2-12x=0\Rightarrow 3x(x-4)=0\Rightarrow x=0 \text{ or } x=4$.

**Differentiate again.**
$\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}=6x-12$.

**Classify with the second-derivative test.**
At $x=0$: $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}=6(0)-12=-12<0$, so $x=0$ is a **local maximum**. Its $y$-value is $y=0-0+5=5$, giving $(0,5)$.
At $x=4$: $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}=6(4)-12=12>0$, so $x=4$ is a **local minimum**. Its $y$-value is $y=64-96+5=-27$, giving $(4,-27)$.

**Final answer:** local maximum at $(0,5)$, local minimum at $(4,-27)$.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks:**

- **M1 — differentiate.** Awarded for attempting the first derivative with powers reduced correctly. A METHOD mark: it stands even if a coefficient later slips.
- **A1 — correct first derivative.** Awarded for $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3x^2-12x$. This accuracy mark depends on the M1 above.
- **M1 — set the derivative to zero and solve.** Awarded for setting THEIR $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=0$ and solving to get $x=0$ and $x=4$. Because it acts on 'their derivative', a candidate who mis-differentiated still earns this method mark for the correct approach.
- **M1 — second derivative.** Awarded for differentiating again to $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}=6x-12$ (or for any valid classification method, e.g. a first-derivative sign check).
- **A1 — classify $x=0$.** Awarded for $f''(0)=-12<0\Rightarrow$ local maximum. Follow-through applies: a candidate using their own (wrong) $x$-value but correctly evaluating and interpreting their $f''$ still earns this.
- **A1 — classify $x=4$.** Awarded for $f''(4)=12>0\Rightarrow$ local minimum, again on a follow-through basis.

**Accept equivalent forms.** The engine marks the mathematics, not the formatting: it accepts the classification stated as 'max/min', as coordinates $(0,5)$ and $(4,-27)$, or justified instead by a first-derivative sign change, and it does not require any fixed wording.

**Bottom line:** of the 6 marks, three are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip, and the two classification marks are shielded by follow-through. A candidate who writes only '$x=0$ max, $x=4$ min' with no derivatives risks losing 4 marks if a value is wrong; a candidate who shows $\frac{dy}{dx}$, solves $\frac{dy}{dx}=0$, shows $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ and evaluates it keeps the method marks regardless.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with follow-through. So always show the method: write $\frac{dy}{dx}$, show $\frac{dy}{dx}=0$ being solved, write $\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$, then evaluate it at each stationary point and state max or min. The method marks and follow-through are what protect your score when a final number slips — and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Using your GDC effectively

Your GDC checks all of this analytic work. Graph $f$ and read off maxima and minima directly, or use the maximum/minimum finder over a sensible interval. The numerical derivative feature gives $f'(a)$ at a point without algebra, and applying it to $f'$ (or graphing $f'$ and finding where it turns) locates where $f''=0$, i.e. candidate inflexion points. Keep the calculator in radian mode for any trig differentiation, and use the graph shape to confirm concavity — cup-shaped where $f''>0$, cap-shaped where $f''<0$.
Where this leads

You now have the full HL differentiation toolkit: the standard functions, the three combination rules, and the second derivative for reading curvature and classifying turning points. This is the machinery behind the modelling questions on Paper 2 — optimising a real quantity, describing where a model accelerates or decelerates, finding where a rate of change is greatest — and it underpins the reverse process, integration, which comes next. Master the habit — differentiate, set $f'=0$, then use $f''$ to classify and to read the shape — and the rest of HL calculus becomes variations on a method you already own.
